ops.title: Pinned section headers via SectionGroup preprocessing
ops.body:
# Pinned section headers via SectionGroup preprocessing

The iOS board screen preprocesses the flat block list into `SectionGroup` runs (in `BoardDerivations.swift`, `sectionGroups`) **before** rendering. This is not an optimization — it is required by SwiftUI's `LazyVStack(pinnedViews:)`, which can only pin a header when the header and its content live inside the same `Section`. A flat `ForEach` over blocks cannot express "this section header pins over the following blocks"; the grouping pass builds one `Section` per header-led run.

## Constraints to preserve

- **Header-led runs**: a `section` block opens a group containing every following block until the next `section` (or end of doc). Blocks before the first `section` form a leading group with no pinned header.
- **ID namespacing**: group IDs use the `"s#" + <section id>` scheme, with a `"lead"` sentinel for the headerless leading group. This prevents `ForEach` key collisions between group IDs and raw block IDs (a section's own block id also appears inside the doc; the `s#` prefix keeps the two namespaces disjoint). If the grouping logic changes, the namespacing must survive — colliding keys break SwiftUI diffing silently (views stop updating or duplicate).
- `BoardDerivationsTests.swift` covers header-led run construction (`sectionGroups`); extend it rather than deleting cases when the grouping strategy changes.

## Why it matters now

The live-revision loop makes mid-review `block.upserted`/`block.removed` first-class: groups are recomputed on every doc change, so stable, collision-free group identity is what keeps SwiftUI animations/diffs sane while Claude rewrites steps under the user.
ops.when: When refactoring board section rendering, reconsidering block grouping strategy, or changing the section ID scheme
